seo.title: Cybersecurity Data Extraction AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for data extraction AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for data extraction in cybersecurity environments introduces unique risks where a single misconfiguration can expose threat intelligence to adversaries, trigger false-positive incident responses, or escalate active security incidents. This checklist ensures your structured output extraction pipelines comply with NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and CISA directives while maintaining the integrity of sensitive threat data. Following these controls is not optional — in cybersecurity, AI deployment failures can directly translate into organizational breaches or operational outages.

sections.heading: Data Handling & Threat Intelligence Isolation
sections.description: Ensure that threat intelligence, IOCs, and classified security data processed by the AI agent are never exposed to external LLM APIs or logged in plaintext, preventing adversary exploitation of leaked intelligence.

items.label: Air-gap or proxy LLM API calls for classified threat data
items.description: Route all LLM API calls through an on-premises or private cloud inference endpoint when processing classified IOCs, TTPs, or threat actor profiles. Never send raw threat intelligence to third-party LLM APIs such as OpenAI or Anthropic public endpoints.

items.label: Implement data masking before schema-guided extraction
items.description: Strip or tokenize sensitive fields (e.g., victim organization names, classified CVE details, internal IP ranges) from unstructured documents before passing them to LLM extraction calls. Restore original values post-extraction using a secure lookup table.

items.label: Enforce output field allowlisting in extraction schemas
items.description: Define strict JSON schemas that only permit extraction of pre-approved fields. Reject any LLM output containing fields not defined in the schema to prevent exfiltration of unintended data through schema drift.

items.label: Disable LLM provider training data opt-in for all API calls
items.description: Confirm via API configuration or data processing agreements that extracted cybersecurity content is not used for model training by the LLM provider. Obtain written DPA confirmation aligned with ISO 27001 Annex A.13.

items.label: Log all extraction inputs and outputs to a SIEM with tamper-evident audit trails
items.description: Forward all agent input documents and structured extraction outputs to your SIEM (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) with cryptographic hashing to ensure audit trail integrity for SOC 2 Type II CC7.2 compliance.

items.label: Apply data retention limits to extraction pipeline caches
items.description: Configure extraction pipeline caches and temporary storage to auto-purge after a defined retention window (e.g., 24 hours for ephemeral threat data), aligned with your ISO 27001 A.8.3 media handling policy.

items.label: Validate that extracted threat data does not appear in LLM error logs or debug outputs
items.description: Audit LLM provider error logs, request traces, and debug outputs monthly to confirm that threat intelligence content is not inadvertently captured in vendor-side logging systems.

sections.heading: Schema Compliance & Extraction Accuracy Validation
sections.description: Structured output extraction must produce schema-compliant, type-consistent, and complete results. Inaccurate extractions in cybersecurity contexts — such as wrong CVE IDs, incorrect severity scores, or missing IOC fields — can directly cause false-positive incident responses or missed detections.

items.label: Enforce strict JSON schema validation on every LLM extraction output
items.description: Use a schema validation library (e.g., Pydantic, AJV, jsonschema) to validate every LLM response against the defined extraction schema before downstream consumption. Reject and quarantine outputs that fail validation rather than passing them forward.

items.label: Implement field-level type consistency checks for all extracted security entities
items.description: Verify that extracted fields match expected types: CVE IDs match the CVE-YYYY-NNNNN regex pattern, IP addresses pass CIDR validation, severity scores are within CVSS 0.0–10.0 range, and timestamps conform to ISO 8601 format.

items.label: Cross-validate extracted CVE identifiers against the NVD and MITRE CVE databases
items.description: After extraction, programmatically query the NVD API (nvd.nist.gov/developers) to confirm that each extracted CVE ID exists and that extracted severity/CVSS scores match the authoritative database record. Flag discrepancies for human review.

items.label: Define and enforce completeness thresholds for required extraction fields
items.description: Specify which schema fields are mandatory (e.g., threat actor name, affected system, CVE ID, severity) and reject extractions where required fields are null or empty. Configure alerting when completeness drops below 95% across a batch.

items.label: Run extraction accuracy regression tests against a labeled cybersecurity document benchmark
items.description: Maintain a curated test set of 100+ labeled cybersecurity documents (threat reports, advisories, vulnerability disclosures) and run automated extraction accuracy tests on every model or prompt update, requiring >90% field-level accuracy before deployment.

items.label: Implement confidence scoring for extracted fields and flag low-confidence outputs
items.description: Prompt the LLM to return a confidence score (0–1) alongside each extracted field. Route extractions with any field confidence below 0.75 to a human analyst queue rather than automated downstream processing.

items.label: Detect and reject hallucinated security entities in extraction outputs
items.description: Implement a post-extraction hallucination check that verifies extracted threat actor names against known threat intelligence databases (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK, VirusTotal) and flags entities with zero external corroboration.

items.label: Version-control all extraction schemas and enforce backward compatibility checks
items.description: Store extraction schemas in version control (e.g., Git) and run automated compatibility tests when schemas are updated to ensure existing downstream consumers (SIEM rules, SOAR playbooks) are not broken by field changes.

sections.heading: Human Oversight & Automated Response Controls
sections.description: AI-driven data extraction feeding into automated security response pipelines must include mandatory human approval gates for critical system actions, preventing automated escalation of security incidents or outages caused by false-positive extractions.

items.label: Require human approval for any automated response action triggered by AI-extracted data on critical systems
items.description: Implement an approval workflow (e.g., via PagerDuty, ServiceNow, or SOAR platform) that pauses automated response actions — such as firewall rule changes, system isolation, or account lockouts — on Tier 1 critical systems until a human analyst confirms the extraction-derived alert.

items.label: Define and enforce a criticality classification for systems subject to AI-driven automated actions
items.description: Maintain a formal asset criticality register that classifies systems as Tier 1 (critical — human approval required), Tier 2 (high — human notification required), or Tier 3 (standard — automated action permitted). Align classifications with CISA Critical Infrastructure sector definitions.

items.label: Implement a dead-man switch to halt automated extraction pipelines during active incidents
items.description: Configure an emergency stop mechanism that allows SOC analysts to immediately pause all AI extraction and automated response pipelines during active security incidents, preventing AI actions from interfering with manual incident response.

items.label: Set rate limits and blast radius controls on automated actions derived from extracted data
items.description: Cap the number of automated response actions (e.g., IP blocks, account suspensions) that can be triggered per hour from AI-extracted data. Require human escalation if the action threshold is exceeded, preventing mass outages from false-positive extraction batches.

items.label: Maintain a human-reviewable audit log of all AI extraction outputs that triggered automated actions
items.description: For every automated security action triggered by AI-extracted data, log the source document, extracted fields, schema validation result, and action taken in a human-readable audit trail accessible to SOC leads and compliance auditors.

items.label: Conduct quarterly tabletop exercises simulating AI extraction failures and false-positive response scenarios
items.description: Run structured tabletop exercises where SOC teams practice responding to scenarios including AI-extracted false CVE data triggering unnecessary system isolation, or leaked threat intelligence causing adversary counter-action. Document lessons learned and update runbooks.

items.label: Establish SLA thresholds for human review of flagged low-confidence extractions
items.description: Define maximum review times for human analyst queues (e.g., critical severity extractions reviewed within 15 minutes, high within 1 hour) and monitor SLA compliance via dashboard. Escalate breached SLAs to SOC management automatically.

sections.heading: Compliance Auditing & Continuous Monitoring
sections.description: AI extraction pipelines in cybersecurity must be continuously monitored for drift, anomalies, and compliance violations, with evidence collection structured to satisfy SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and NIST CSF audit requirements.

items.label: Map AI extraction pipeline controls to NIST CSF functions and document evidence for each
items.description: Create a control mapping document linking each pipeline component (data ingestion, LLM call, schema validation, output routing) to NIST CSF Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover functions. Collect and store evidence artifacts quarterly for auditor review.

items.label: Conduct SOC 2 Type II evidence collection for AI extraction pipeline access controls
items.description: Document and collect evidence for logical access controls on the extraction pipeline, including role-based access to extraction schemas, API key management, and privileged access reviews, aligned with SOC 2 CC6.1–CC6.3 common criteria.

items.label: Implement real-time anomaly detection on extraction pipeline outputs for data exfiltration patterns
items.description: Deploy behavioral analytics (e.g., via UEBA tools or custom SIEM rules) to detect anomalous extraction patterns such as unusually large output volumes, extraction of fields outside normal distribution, or outputs being routed to unexpected destinations.

items.label: Perform monthly ISO 27001 A.12.4 compliant log reviews of all AI extraction pipeline activity
items.description: Schedule monthly reviews of extraction pipeline logs covering API call volumes, schema validation failure rates, human approval queue metrics, and data routing events. Document review outcomes and remediation actions in your ISMS.

items.label: Track and report AI extraction pipeline KPIs to security leadership monthly
items.description: Report monthly metrics including extraction accuracy rate, schema validation failure rate, human approval queue SLA compliance, false-positive rate for triggered alerts, and any CISA directive compliance gaps to CISO and security leadership.

items.label: Conduct annual penetration testing specifically targeting the AI extraction pipeline
items.description: Commission annual red team exercises that specifically attempt to exploit the AI extraction pipeline — including prompt injection attacks on LLM calls, schema bypass attempts, and data exfiltration via extraction output channels. Remediate all critical findings within 30 days.

items.label: Maintain a model and prompt change management process with security impact assessments
items.description: Require a formal security impact assessment before any change to LLM model versions, extraction prompts, or schema definitions. Assess changes for potential accuracy regression, hallucination risk increase, or new data leakage vectors before production deployment.

items.label: Review and update CISA directive compliance posture for AI systems semi-annually
items.description: Semi-annually review applicable CISA Emergency Directives and Binding Operational Directives for any new requirements affecting AI systems, automated response tools, or threat intelligence handling, and update pipeline controls accordingly within mandated timeframes.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: NIST CSF RS.AN-5 requires that threat intelligence used in automated response be validated for accuracy before action — AI-extracted threat data must pass schema validation and CVE cross-reference checks before feeding into any SOAR playbook or automated response workflow.

SOC 2 Type II CC6.6 mandates that data transmitted to external parties (including LLM API providers) be protected against unauthorized access — ensure all cybersecurity document content sent to LLM APIs is encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and that vendor data processing agreements explicitly prohibit use of your data for model training.

ISO 27001 Annex A.14.2.8 requires that security testing be integrated into development pipelines — AI extraction pipeline deployments must include automated schema validation tests, hallucination detection checks, and prompt injection resistance tests as mandatory CI/CD pipeline gates before any production release.

CISA BOD 23-01 and related directives on asset visibility and vulnerability enumeration require that AI-extracted CVE and vulnerability data be reconciled with authoritative sources (NVD, CISA KEV catalog) within defined timeframes — implement automated reconciliation jobs that flag discrepancies between AI-extracted severity scores and CISA KEV catalog entries within 24 hours of extraction.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI extraction pipelines against NIST CSF, SOC 2, and ISO 27001 controls — catching schema drift, data leakage risks, and compliance gaps before they become incidents.
